likely_position: Counsel would most likely treat HCD's notice of violation as the highest-probability loss — the 25-year covenant term (short of the ~45-55 year durations required under Gov. Code § 54221(f)/(g) exemption categories) and the affordability mix make the "exempt surplus land" claim weak — and would recommend curing rather than litigating: pause performance, negotiate a compliance/workout agreement with HCD (extending covenants, revisiting the mix, and/or restarting notice-of-availability procedures under §§ 54222–54223 with housing sponsors) to avoid the 30% penalty exposure. On the gift-clause claim, counsel would likely defend rather than settle, citing the public-purpose doctrine and in-kind consideration (affordable units, public plaza) as adequate under cases like *County of Alameda v. Janssen* and the deference courts give to legislative findings of public benefit, viewing this as the lowest-risk exposure. On the local-preference covenant, counsel would likely get ahead of the threatened FHA/FEHA suit proactively — narrowing the five-year preference (lower than 50%, or reframe as displacement/anti-displacement based rather than broad residency-based) before litigation is filed, since disparate-impact exposure given the city's demographics (per *Texas Dep't of Housing v. Inclusive Communities Project*) is seen as real but curable by redesign. Overall, the likely institutional posture is: cure the SLA problem administratively with HCD, defend the gift-clause claim in litigation if sued, and quietly amend the preference covenant to reduce fair-housing risk — all while advising the council that the deal is salvageable through DDA amendment rather than rescission.
